Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Victoria Welby

1837 – 1912

The founder of significs: meaning, translation, and the expressive life of mind.

A preliminary correction is worth making gently: Lady Victoria Welby was a woman, so I will refer to her as “she.” She also did not write a single, canonical treatise called “a philosophy of mind.” What she developed was Significs: a general theory of significance, interpretation, expression, translation, language, value, and communication. But because she repeatedly treats meaning, expression, consciousness, mental evolution, sensation, intention, conduct, mysticism, and “soul” vocabulary, her work can be reconstructed as a distinctive philosophy of mind. The reconstruction is not artificial; it follows from her own insistence that the interpretive function is central to “every possible sphere of human interest and purpose,” and that the neglect of meaning and interpretation had distorted logic, psychology, philosophy, education, and religion.

The central thesis of this report is that Welby’s implicit philosophy of mind is a signific, evolutionary, process-oriented, anti-Cartesian philosophy of mind. Mind is not primarily an inner substance, a private theater, or an immaterial ego standing over against matter. Mind is the developing activity by which organisms, and pre-eminently human beings, respond to signs, translate experience, express intention, and rise from mere sensation to meaning and finally to significance. In one of her most revealing formulations, she asks: “what is Mind … but the moment in which the world takes on Meaning and its expression?”

1. Textual basis and interpretive caution

Welby’s principal mature works for this reconstruction are What is Meaning? Studies in the Development of Significance (1903) and Significs and Language: The Articulate Form of Our Expressive and Interpretative Resources (1911). The latter explicitly says it is not a continuous systematic treatise but a collection of short papers written over several years, so any “Welbyan philosophy of mind” must be reconstructed across scattered essays, notes, correspondence, and later scholarship.

That said, the reconstruction is strongly grounded. Welby herself frames Significs as a discipline concerned with the “nature of Significance in all its forms and relations,” and with the working of significance in every sphere of human purpose. She also says that the interpretive function has been neglected despite being a condition of human intercourse and of humanity’s mastery of the world. That makes Significs, among other things, a theory of mindedness: a theory of how creatures make, receive, transform, and evaluate meaning.

Historically, Welby was not marginal in the sense of isolation, even though she was long neglected. She corresponded with major figures including C. S. Peirce, Bertrand Russell, F. C. S. Schiller, William James, and others; she published What is Meaning? and Significs and Language; and later scholars identify her as a pioneering figure in semiotics, pragmatism, philosophy of language, translation theory, and early analytic-philosophical developments.

2. The basic problem: psychology without significance is incomplete

Welby’s philosophy of mind begins from a diagnostic claim: modern intellectual culture had accumulated enormous knowledge but had failed to understand meaning itself. She argues that “meaning,” “significance,” and “interpretation” are not secondary ornaments added to cognition; they are conditions of intelligent life. A fact matters to intelligence because it has meaning. Yet she complains that the cardinal importance of meaning had been ignored or trivialized, even in logic, psychology, and philosophy.

This is crucial. Welby is not merely giving a theory of words. She is objecting to an entire model of mind in which cognition is treated as if it were the passive registration of facts, the manipulation of definitions, or the possession of inner ideas. For her, the mind is an interpretive power. Its success or failure depends on whether it can discriminate sense, intention, tendency, relevance, implication, emotional force, and value. This is why she repeatedly connects Significs with education: people must be trained to detect “sense, meaning, and significance,” including tendency, intention, and essential interest.

She is equally suspicious of the idea that better definitions alone will solve mental confusion. Definition matters, but ambiguity is not simply a defect to be eliminated. Language is organically ambiguous because life, context, and intention are variable. Meaning is sensitive to what she calls the “psychological climate.” Thus, mental clarity does not consist in freezing language into rigid formulas; it consists in developing interpretive tact.

From a philosophy-of-mind standpoint, this places Welby against any view that treats cognition as purely formal, atomistic, or private. Mind is not only representational; it is contextual, purposive, affective, communicative, and value-sensitive.

3. The triadic structure of mind: sense, meaning, significance

The most important architecture in Welby’s thought is her triad of sense, meaning, and significance. She often maps this triad onto related terms: signification, intention, and ideal value; or instinctive, volitional, and moral aspects of mental life. Later commentators summarize the triad as a movement from primitive or pre-rational sense, through rational and intentional meaning, toward significance as import, value, relevance, responsibility, and ultimate bearing.

The first level, sense, is not merely linguistic sense. It includes sense-experience, bodily orientation, “making sense,” practical apprehension, and the pre-reflective intelligibility of the world. Welby explicitly denies that there is simply “the Sense of a word” in abstraction; there is the sense in which a word is used, dependent on circumstance, state of mind, reference, and universe of discourse.

The second level, meaning, is tied to intention. Meaning is not just dictionary content; it is the intent, aim, or purposive direction of an utterance, action, or expression. This gives Welby a deeply intentional theory of mind, but not in the narrow later sense of mental “aboutness” alone. Meaning involves will, purpose, coherence, and directedness.

The third level, significance, is the richest. It includes emotional force, ideal value, moral and social range, and the larger consequences of words and acts. Science, logic, philosophy, aesthetics, ethics, and religion are, for Welby, all concentrated around the work of sense, meaning, and significance.

This triad yields a layered philosophy of mind:

Level Mental function Philosophical significance
Sense Sensory, practical, contextual apprehension Mind begins in embodied responsiveness, not abstract representation.
Meaning Intention, purpose, coherent use Mind is purposive and expressive; it means by intending and acting.
Significance Value, implication, responsibility, ideal bearing Mature mind evaluates, transforms, and situates meaning within life as a whole.

The key point is that Welby does not reduce higher mind to lower sensation, nor does she sever higher reason from embodied sense. She sees continuity and ascent. Sensation, instinct, expression, intention, reason, morality, and spirituality form a developmental scale.

4. Mind as expression, not merely inner representation

Welby’s account of mind is expressive. The mind does not first exist as a sealed inward realm and then accidentally attach signs to its contents. Rather, mind becomes articulate through expression, response, and interpretation. She says that one cannot raise the level of expression without raising thought and imagination. She also treats the function of expression and response as underdeveloped and argues that greater powers of mental communication are within reach if people attend to them.

This is one of the strongest anti-Cartesian strands in her philosophy. The Cartesian picture tends to begin with a thinking subject who has private mental contents and must then solve the problem of how those contents relate to bodies, signs, and other minds. Welby begins elsewhere: with expression, response, sign-use, interpretation, and communicative life. Mind is not a hidden object behind expression; expression is one of mind’s modes of being.

She also extends this beyond speech. Action translates thought into deed, and even the “man of action” can fail if he grasps direct use but misses wider significance. A sign’s value is not exhausted by immediate reference. Conduct itself is signific.

This allows us to state a central Welbyan thesis:

Mental life is the power of significant expression and interpretation across sensation, language, action, emotion, social relation, and value.

That thesis makes her relevant to later philosophy of language, pragmatism, semiotics, embodied cognition, enactivism, and social theories of mind, even though she did not use those later labels.

5. Mind as translation: cognition, emotion, and conduct

Welby’s concept of translation is indispensable for extracting her philosophy of mind. She does not mean translation only between languages. Translation is a universal operation of mind and life: transformation, transposition, interpretation, conversion from one expressive medium or level into another. She even calls translation “mental digestion,” emphasizing that the mind does not merely receive signs; it assimilates, transforms, and re-expresses them.

This is a powerful cognitive model. Perception translates stimulus into sense. Thought translates sense into meaning. Action translates intention into deed. Education translates inherited powers into articulate capacities. Ethics translates impulse into better forms of conduct. Religion and mysticism must be translated into terms that can survive scientific and moral scrutiny.

Welby explicitly rejects the myth that there is always a “plain meaning” available to everyone without interpretation. A so-called plain meaning may exist only in a loose, superficial sense. Serious understanding requires translation among contexts, associations, intentions, and values.

Her discussion of emotional signs is especially relevant to psychology. She says that emotional signs can be translated, and that the task is not merely to eradicate undesirable tendencies but to translate them into corresponding good. This gives her something like a moral-psychological theory of sublimation or transformation: mental development does not consist simply in repression, but in interpretive conversion.

Here Welby anticipates later views in which cognition is not a static mirror but an active, transformative process. She also anticipates therapeutic and educational models in which harmful affective patterns must be reinterpreted and redirected rather than merely suppressed.

6. Evolutionary psychology: continuity from organism to human mind

Welby’s philosophy of mind is deeply evolutionary. In her 1890 essay “An Apparent Paradox in Mental Evolution,” she asks whether the facts of mental development can be accounted for through “unbroken continuity” from earliest to latest forms. She treats life as a process of appropriate reaction to stimulus, development, preservation, and reproduction. Organism and environment are in practical relation; the world impresses practical meaning on living beings, and failure of response has consequences.

This gives Welby a proto-enactive account of mind. Mind does not arise in a vacuum. It emerges from organism-environment coordination: response, adjustment, correction, survival, and increasing complexity. The senses help relate notions and actions rightly to environment. Higher mental activity depends on a continuing correspondence between thing and thought, though emotion can misdirect this correspondence by setting the wrong “mental muscles” in motion.

Her question is whether human mentality represents a break in evolution or a higher continuity. She leans toward continuity. She suggests that there may be no absolute chasm, only ignorance, lack of observation, lack of attention, or failure to call up reactive power from lower strata of consciousness or below consciousness.

This is not reductive materialism. Welby does not say that mind is “nothing but” physiology. But neither does she accept a sharp metaphysical gap between life and mind. She wants an evolutionary ascent from organic sign-response to intentional symbol interpretation and ultimately to disciplined significance. Human beings are distinctive not because they escape nature but because they intensify nature’s expressive and interpretive powers.

7. Consciousness, subconsciousness, and the whole person

Welby’s remarks on consciousness are scattered, but they point to a layered, whole-person model. She notes that philosophical vocabulary often confuses “mind,” “consciousness,” “will,” “feeling,” and “thought,” using them interchangeably without adequate discrimination. This is important because she is not satisfied with loose introspective psychology. She wants a more exact signific analysis of mental terms.

One of her most suggestive passages connects anatomical bases of consciousness and memory with the “whole person.” She says that the highest nervous centers represent the whole person physically, while consciousness represents the whole person psychically. She then suggests that this may be the basis or starting point for a further layer of personality, invoking St. Paul’s phrase about spiritual life and speaking of a “spiritual living Identity” potentially involving the whole organism, individual, or even race.

Several claims follow.

First, consciousness is not a detached point-like ego. It is the psychical representation of the whole person.

Second, the physical and psychical are parallel aspects of an integrated organism, not two sealed substances accidentally connected.

Third, personality may have layers: bodily organism, psychical consciousness, and spiritual identity.

Fourth, the “spiritual” is not simply opposed to the bodily. Welby’s spiritual language is layered upon organism and consciousness rather than substituted for them.

This makes her view neither standard substance dualism nor standard materialism. It is better described as integrative, developmental, and processual.

8. Rejection of the crude matter/mind antithesis

Welby explicitly warns against allowing the old antithesis of “matter and mind” to coerce inquiry. She suggests that reality may be monistic with respect to ultimate division or separation, while also being dualistic, triadic, or pluralistic from different viewpoints. This is a remarkably flexible metaphysical position: she resists both simplistic dualism and simplistic reduction.

This matters for the philosophy of mind because the dominant early modern problem was often framed as: how can immaterial mind interact with material body? Welby’s answer is not to solve that problem on its inherited terms. She tries to dissolve and transform it. The opposition between matter and mind is itself a problematic sign-system, a conceptual inheritance whose significance must be reinterpreted.

Her likely view is something like this: reality is one in ultimate process, but it appears under many aspects depending on interpretive level. Matter, life, mind, language, value, and spirit are not unrelated substances but phases or organizations of meaningful process.

This view becomes clearer when placed beside recent scholarship on her metaphysics. Emily Thomas’s study describes Welby as developing a metaphysical system centered on Motion, her “supreme category.” On that reading, material bodies, living beings, and conscious minds are all complexes of motion. A review of Thomas’s book further explains that Welby’s “Motion idealism” treats Motion as Spirit or foundational non-material energy, giving her a nuanced form of panpsychism while still struggling to reconcile pervasive mind-activity with life and consciousness.

So the best reconstruction is not “Welby believed in an immaterial soul separate from the body,” nor “Welby reduced mind to matter.” It is: Welby interpreted mind as a higher, signific phase of a dynamic reality continuous with life, expression, and value.

9. Panpsychism, process metaphysics, and semiotic cosmology

Modern scholarship increasingly reads Welby as more metaphysically ambitious than older accounts suggested. Thomas presents her as a philosopher of Motion, idealism, panpsychism, change, space, time, and Darwinian continuity. Panpsychism, in the broad philosophical sense, is the view that mind or mind-like being is a fundamental and pervasive feature of the universe, though versions differ over whether the relevant mental feature is consciousness, proto-consciousness, experience, or some more basic mind-like property.

Welby’s version should be handled carefully. It would be misleading to call her a panpsychist in a simple contemporary sense if that means every particle has consciousness like a tiny subject. Her position is better reconstructed as pansemiotic or signific-process panpsychism: the world is structured by motion, expression, response, and interpretability, and mind is the intensified, reflective, value-bearing form of a more general signific process.

A reviewer of Thomas’s work makes exactly this kind of interpretive move, arguing that for Welby Significs was not merely philosophy of language but a universal method for phenomena from unconscious cellular response to the cosmos. On this reading, her system becomes an evolutionary cosmology of a semiotic conception of mind, with inorganic, organic, and mental levels forming a hierarchy of increasing signification and interpretation.

This is one of the most fertile places to locate Welby historically. She belongs near, but not simply inside, several traditions:

She is near idealism because reality is not understood as brute matter devoid of significance.

She is near panpsychism because mind-like or interpretive features are not confined to adult human consciousness.

She is near process philosophy because Motion, development, transformation, and translation are basic.

She is near semiotics because signs, interpretation, and meaning are central to mind and world.

She is near pragmatism because meaning is inseparable from use, action, purpose, and consequence.

Yet she is reducible to none of these labels.

10. “Mother-sense,” subjectivity, and the pre-rational basis of mind

Welby’s concept of mother-sense is also important for her philosophy of mind. Later scholarship describes mother-sense as a generating matrix of signification, experience, language, knowledge, consciousness, and worldview. It is not irrationality opposed to reason; rather, rationalizing intellect presupposes it. Welby’s contrast between mother-sense and father-reason, or sense and intellect, is meant to recover both intuitive and rational knowledge as integrated powers.

This gives her an account of cognition broader than formal reasoning. Human understanding is not born from abstract logic alone. It grows from practical sense, embodied responsiveness, affective attunement, maternal or generative intuition, and social inheritance. “Reason” is a later articulation of more primitive but indispensable sense-making powers.

This has several philosophical consequences.

First, Welby rejects a hyper-intellectualist model of mind. Thought is not exhausted by propositional judgment.

Second, she gives dignity to pre-reflective and affective intelligence.

Third, she treats language and consciousness as rooted in a larger matrix of life and relation.

Fourth, she anticipates later critiques of disembodied rationalism, especially those that stress care, relation, affect, and embodied social cognition.

Her notes on subjectivity also point in a dialogical direction. Scholarship reports that she distinguished “I” and “self,” and treated identity as multiplex, polyvocal, and dialogic, with awareness including otherness. This again distances her from a simple isolated-subject model of mind.

11. Social mind: communication, intersubjectivity, and otherness

Welby’s mind is fundamentally communicative. Signs are not private possessions; they operate in interpretation, translation, and social exchange. Susan Petrilli characterizes Welby’s significs as deeply concerned with meaning, signs, signifiers, and listeners as they evolve in living communication, and emphasizes the social, intersubjective, intercorporeal, dialogic, and value-laden dimensions of signs.

This has major implications for philosophy of mind. Welby does not construct mind from an isolated first-person perspective and then add other minds as an external problem. Instead, mental life is already interpretive and communicative. Understanding involves speaker, listener, sign, context, intention, and significance. The “other mind” is not an afterthought; otherness is internal to the very structure of signification.

This makes her especially relevant to later anti-solipsistic approaches: pragmatism, semiotics, dialogical philosophy, social externalism, and ordinary-language philosophy. She would likely regard the “problem of other minds,” in its strict Cartesian form, as partly the result of a bad initial abstraction. We do not first encounter sealed mental substances; we encounter expressive beings in shared worlds of signs and conduct.

12. Error, confusion, and pathology as failures of interpretation

Welby’s philosophy of mind also implies a theory of mental error. Error is not only false belief. It can be defective translation, misplaced emotional response, confused metaphor, dead language, rigid definition, or failure to grasp significance.

In her account of mental evolution, she discusses how emotional experience can set the wrong “mental muscles” in motion. In What is Meaning?, she repeatedly argues that ambiguity can become confusion if unrecognized, and that people often mistake superficial meaning for true significance.

This gives her a broad diagnostic psychology. Mental failure can occur at each level of the triad:

At the level of sense, one may fail to apprehend context.

At the level of meaning, one may misread intention.

At the level of significance, one may miss value, consequence, moral bearing, or long-range import.

This also explains why education is central to her project. The mind must be trained in interpretive discrimination. For Welby, progress in psychology, ethics, religion, and social life depends not merely on more facts but on better powers of significance.

13. Religion, mysticism, soul, and incorporeal mind

The question of the soul and incorporeal minds requires special care. Welby wrote within a religiously saturated Victorian context, and scholarship identifies mysticism, spiritualism, religion, and science as relevant to her significs. There is also a known 1891 essay by her titled “Breath and the Name of the Soul.” I found bibliographic evidence for that essay, but not enough accessible primary text to ground detailed claims about its contents here.

From the accessible evidence, Welby does not appear to defend a straightforward Cartesian doctrine of an immaterial soul-substance. She does use spiritual language and speaks of a “spiritual living Identity,” but she embeds this in a layered account of organism, consciousness, and personality. The highest nervous centers represent the person physically; consciousness represents the person psychically; and a further spiritual identity may be understood as a higher layer or development.

Her treatment of mysticism is similarly disciplined. She says that the highest human form of organic sign-appreciation becomes a kind of verified and disciplined mysticism that has passed through the “ordeal of science.” She also suggests that mysticism has often been raw material or a forerunner of science, as in the historical relation of alchemy to chemistry.

This is not secular dismissal, but neither is it uncritical supernaturalism. Welby’s mature position seems to be that religious, mystical, and soul-language must be translated, interpreted, and tested for significance. The right question is not simply, “Does an incorporeal mind exist as a separate entity?” but also: What does this term express? What experience does it translate? What practical, moral, and spiritual significance does it carry? What confusions does it inherit? What does science require us to revise?

Therefore, the most defensible reconstruction is:

Welby preserves the spiritual dimension of mind while refusing to isolate it as a dogmatic immaterial substance. “Soul” names a region of significance, identity, aspiration, and spiritual interpretation, not an explanatory posit separable from the whole organism-person.

Her view can accommodate the thought that mind or spirit is more pervasive than human bodily consciousness, especially in her Motion metaphysics and panpsychist tendencies. But it is not a conventional doctrine of incorporeal individual minds floating free of embodiment. It is a signific reinterpretation of spiritual vocabulary within an evolutionary, semiotic, and scientific framework.

14. Primitive mind, myth, animism, and the “soul” in anthropology

Welby’s work on mental evolution engages topics that nineteenth-century anthropology associated with animism, myth, ghosts, shadows, reflections, and soul-beliefs. In “An Apparent Paradox in Mental Evolution,” she discusses questions around ghosts, reflections, “meat-shadows,” and why certain bodily terms such as “blood” may or may not become names for “soul.”

Her deeper interest is not antiquarian. She is asking how the human mind moves from sense-bound experience to non-sensuous meaning. She asks whether language ambiguity leads modern interpreters to attribute overly abstract ideas to what she calls the “primitive sense-bound mind,” and she looks for a bridge between sensuous and non-sensuous interpretation.

The period vocabulary here is dated and should be treated critically. But philosophically, the issue is important: Welby is studying how “soul,” “spirit,” and other invisible entities may arise through translation from bodily, sensory, and environmental signs. In this respect, her view belongs to the history of theories of animism and religious cognition, but with a distinctive signific twist. She is not merely explaining religion away as error. She argues that grotesque or archaic ceremonies may be failed translations of deep truths, and that the task is to interpret myth, ritual, religion, and mysticism without reducing them to dogmatic theories.

Thus, soul-language is historically and psychologically significant because it shows the mind translating visible signs into invisible meanings. The philosophical question becomes: when is that translation confused, and when does it disclose genuine significance?

15. Welby’s place in the development of philosophy of mind

Welby occupies an unusual but important historical position. She stands at the intersection of late-Victorian evolutionary theory, anthropology, psychology, religious modernism, semiotics, pragmatism, philosophy of language, and early analytic philosophy.

In relation to nineteenth-century psychology, she belongs to the era when “mind” was still discussed across physiology, consciousness studies, anthropology, education, and moral philosophy. Her attention to nervous centers, consciousness, instinct, subconscious strata, emotional response, and mental evolution places her within that broad psychological field.

In relation to semiotics and pragmatism, she is a major precursor. Petrilli describes her as a “mother-founder” of modern semiotics and emphasizes her shared interpretive orientation with Peirce. Welby’s concern with signs, values, consequences, and living communication makes her an important figure in the pragmatist-semiotic genealogy of mind.

In relation to philosophy of language, she anticipates the linguistic turn. Her work on ambiguity, use, interpretation, metaphor, and the impossibility of context-free “plain meaning” foreshadows later concerns in ordinary-language philosophy and analytic philosophy, even though her style and metaphysical interests are very different from later analytic orthodoxy.

In relation to contemporary philosophy of mind, she is best read as an ancestor of several anti-Cartesian tendencies: embodied cognition, enactivism, social externalism, affective cognition, biosemiotics, process philosophy, and renewed interest in panpsychism. She does not give a modern technical theory of neural computation, qualia, or representational content. Instead, she offers a broader theory of mindedness as living interpretation.

Her originality is that she treats mind, language, and value together. Many later theories separate them: philosophy of mind studies consciousness, philosophy of language studies meaning, ethics studies value. Welby’s Significs sees these as inseparable aspects of significance.

16. Systematic reconstruction: the Welbyan philosophy of mind

A comprehensive reconstruction of Welby’s philosophy of mind can be stated in the following theses.

1. Mind is signific activity.
Mind is the activity through which the world becomes sensed, meant, interpreted, expressed, translated, and valued. It is not merely inner consciousness but the living power of significance.

2. Mind is embodied and organismic.
Human consciousness represents the whole person psychically, while the nervous system represents the person physically. Mental life is rooted in organismic response, sensation, and bodily orientation.

3. Mind is continuous with life.
The difference between organic response and human thought is developmental, not an absolute metaphysical rupture. The human mind intensifies processes already present in organism-environment relation: response, adjustment, interpretation, and transformation.

4. Mind is triadic.
Mental life develops through sense, meaning, and significance. Sense gives contextual apprehension; meaning gives intention; significance gives value, implication, and ideal bearing.

5. Mind is expressive.
Thought and imagination rise with expression. Action translates thought into deed. Language, gesture, conduct, and symbolic forms are not external containers of inner mental contents; they are forms in which mind realizes itself.

6. Mind is translative.
Cognition is not passive mirroring but translation: mental digestion, transformation, and re-expression. Emotions, instincts, myths, words, actions, and spiritual experiences all require translation.

7. Mind is social and dialogic.
Meaning emerges in communication among sign, speaker, listener, context, and value. Subjectivity is not isolated; it includes otherness and develops through intercorporeal and intersubjective sign-processes.

8. Mind is normative.
The highest level of mind is not mere information-processing but significance: relevance, responsibility, ideal value, moral bearing, and transformative consequence. A mind fails not only when it believes falsely but when it misreads significance.

9. Mind is spiritually open but not crudely dualist.
Welby allows for spiritual identity, disciplined mysticism, and possibly a pervasive mind-like dimension of reality, but she resists the crude opposition of matter and mind. Soul-language must be interpreted significatively, not merely repeated as dogma.

10. Mind belongs to a dynamic universe.
In her broader metaphysics, reality is motion, transformation, and process. Material bodies, living beings, and conscious minds are complexes of motion, and significance may be a cosmic as well as human principle.

17. Tensions and unresolved problems

Welby’s philosophy of mind is powerful but not without tensions.

The first tension is systematicity. She offers a rich network of insights rather than a formal system. This is partly why she has been historically hard to classify. Her own Significs and Language warns that it is not a systematic treatise.

The second tension is terminological instability. She criticizes the confused use of “mind,” “consciousness,” “will,” “feeling,” and “thought,” yet her own vocabulary can also be fluid. This is not merely a defect; it reflects her conviction that language is living and that rigid definition is insufficient. But it complicates systematic reconstruction.

The third tension is monism versus plurality. She rejects the old matter/mind antithesis and leans toward monism, yet she also speaks in dualistic, triadic, and pluralistic ways depending on viewpoint. This may be a sophisticated perspectival metaphysics, but it also leaves open how exactly physical, psychical, and spiritual layers relate.

The fourth tension is panpsychism versus emergence. If Motion, Spirit, or significance pervades reality, then mind-like process may be everywhere. But if human consciousness is a developed achievement, then mind also emerges in degrees. Welby’s position seems to combine both: significance is cosmically rooted, but articulate human mind is developmentally achieved.

The fifth tension is Victorian anthropology. Her discussions of early religion, primitive mind, and “savage” mentality belong to a historical context whose categories are often morally and scientifically problematic. The lasting philosophical value lies not in those period classifications but in her deeper question: how do minds translate sensory, bodily, and social signs into invisible meanings, myths, values, and spiritual identities?

18. Final assessment

Victoria Welby’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a philosophy of signific mind. Mind is not a detached inner substance; it is the living, embodied, expressive, interpretive, translative, social, and value-bearing activity by which reality becomes meaningful. It begins in organic responsiveness, rises through sense and intention, and reaches maturity in significance: the grasp of value, consequence, and ideal import.

Her view of the soul and incorporeal mind is neither eliminative nor conventionally dualist. She preserves spiritual identity and disciplined mysticism, and her metaphysics of Motion may support a broad panpsychist or spirit-infused cosmos. But she subjects all such language to Significs. “Soul,” for Welby, must be understood as a term requiring translation: from breath, body, consciousness, myth, religion, and aspiration into a more exact account of personality, value, and spiritual significance.

Historically, this places her in a neglected but important alternative lineage in philosophy of mind: not the line from Descartes to mind-body dualism to contemporary consciousness debates alone, but a line running through evolutionary psychology, semiotics, pragmatism, philosophy of language, religious modernism, and process metaphysics. Her contribution is to show that mind cannot be adequately understood apart from signs, expression, interpretation, translation, and value.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
